scenarios.task: We're developing an AI radiology triage device that flags two conditions. There's no cleared predicate device with our exact combined intended use, only one that covers a single condition. Our regulatory lead wants to file a 510(k) against that partial-match predicate because it's the cheapest, fastest-looking path. What's your read?
criteria: Identifies this as a De Novo pathway candidate rather than defaulting to the 510(k) because no true predicate with matching intended use exists
Runs an expected-cost or expected-timeline comparison between the two paths, including the rejection/refile risk on the mismatched-predicate 510(k), with actual numbers
Notes the De Novo path also establishes a new product code / classification advantage, not just a cost/time comparison

scenarios.task: A post-approval firmware update changes how our device's alarm threshold is calculated, but doesn't change the labeled indication. Our engineering team wants to file it as a 'letter to file' since it's just a firmware tweak and that's faster. Should we sign off on that?
criteria: Says a safety-critical software function change requires running the actual change-control decision tree rather than defaulting to letter-to-file
Flags that changing the alarm threshold calculation likely affects a safety-critical function, which pushes toward a new submission/supplement rather than letter-to-file
States the decision needs a documented rationale in the Design History File, not just an engineering judgment call
